lane: Lane 1 v4(a) — in-register SIMD reduction in the VNNI microkernel — REGRESSION, REVERTED
status: REVERTED — not committed. Negative receipt kept so this is not re-attempted.
what_was_tried: Replace the VNNI microkernel's per-block reduction (store the 16-lane __m512i to [i32;16] on the stack, then 4 scalar pair-sums) with an IN-REGISTER reduction: _mm512_extracti64x4 + _mm256_add + _mm_hadd x2 + cvtepi32_ps + scale, no memory store. Bit-identical (property test PASS across all 3 microkernels).
hypothesis: Strictly fewer instructions (no 64-byte store + scalar reads) => faster.
result: REGRESSION. Hardened paired sweep (camelid bench-owner-sweep, median-of-8, vnni4x8/off ratio — comparable across binaries since it normalizes vs the unchanged off path):

measured.control: owner_avx2/off (UNCHANGED code) held steady 1.049->1.046 / 1.064->1.061, confirming the runs are comparable and the regression is real, not drift.

why: The 'strictly fewer ops' intuition failed on this microarchitecture: the in-register reduction is a long DEPENDENT chain (extract -> add -> hadd -> hadd -> unpack -> cvt -> mul -> mul -> load -> add -> store) with high-latency _mm_hadd_epi32 and an AVX-512 <-> AVX2 (256-bit) transition per lane. The store+scalar version lets the CPU retire the store and pipeline the independent scalar int-adds (and the compiler autovectorizes them), which is faster here. Fewer instructions != fewer cycles when the critical path lengthens.
decision: REVERTED. The store+scalar reduction stays. The VNNI microkernel is at a local optimum on this box.
value: Demonstrates why the hardened in-process PAIRED harness was necessary: it caught a plausible 'optimization' that is actually a -3.5% regression, which the noisy cross-invocation A/B could never have resolved.
